behaviors.description: The speech-to-text output preserves speaker labels so that the summary can attribute statements to specific attendees.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that speaker labels are lost or not parsed correctly from the speech-to-text API response. A mock API using a documented response format would hide failures if: (1) the real API's diarization format differs from the mock, (2) the real API doesn't support speaker labels in the way expected, or (3) the parsing code handles the documented format but fails on the actual format. Testing with the real speech-to-text API is required to ensure the code correctly handles the actual response structure and preserves labels end-to-end.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real speech-to-text API with a multi-speaker audio sample or transcript segment where multiple distinct speakers make statements. Verify that the API response includes speaker labels (e.g., diarization enabled). Confirm that these labels are correctly parsed and forwarded through the pipeline to the LLM input without loss or corruption. Validate that speaker attribution is present for each statement reaching the summarizer.

behaviors.description: The structured summary contains at least one action item for every attendee who was assigned a task during the meeting.
behaviors.justification: The failure here is that the real LLM does not correctly extract or attribute action items. Mocking the LLM to return a perfect summary would hide the actual failure: the LLM either misses action items, misattributes them to the wrong person, or fails to extract them from natural language. This is fundamentally non-deterministic—different LLM runs, models, or prompts may produce different results. The minimum level to catch this failure is to test with the real LLM on realistic transcripts where action items are explicitly assigned.
behaviors.test_description: Create a transcript where specific attendees are clearly assigned tasks (e.g., 'Alice will send the report by Friday', 'Bob to schedule the follow-up'). Call the real LLM with the full meeting transcript and a prompt to extract action items with attendee attribution. Verify that the returned structured output contains an action item for each person assigned a task, with correct attributions. Run multiple times to account for LLM variability; if any run is missing an action item that should be present, the test fails.

behaviors.description: Key decisions mentioned in the transcript appear in the "decisions" section of the summary, not buried in the narrative.
behaviors.justification: The failure is that the real LLM either does not identify decisions correctly, does not place them in the structured "decisions" field, or buries them in the narrative sections. Mocking the LLM to return perfectly structured output would hide real LLM behavior. While a code-level test could verify that the output structure is correct (if filled), it cannot verify that the LLM actually extracts and places decisions where required. This depends on real LLM reasoning and instruction-following, which varies across models and prompts.
behaviors.test_description: Create a transcript containing clear decision statements (e.g., 'We decided to migrate to PostgreSQL', 'The team agreed to adopt Rust for the backend'). Call the real LLM with the transcript and a prompt specifying that decisions must be extracted and placed in a "decisions" field of the JSON output. Verify that the returned structure has a non-empty "decisions" field and that the extracted decisions are present in that field, not in narrative sections. Confirm decisions are not duplicated across both fields.

behaviors.description: When the audio file is longer than 60 minutes, the service splits it into segments and processes them sequentially, merging the results into a single coherent summary.
behaviors.justification: The core failure here is in the segmentation and merging logic: the file is not split correctly, or the merged summary loses information, has duplication, or becomes incoherent. This can be caught at Integration level by testing the segmentation module, the processing pipeline (with mocked speech-to-text and LLM), and the merging module together. The failure of the real API or LLM to handle the merged context well is a secondary concern (Agentic/Workflow). Testing at Integration with a long transcript (>60 minutes) and mocks for external APIs is sufficient to catch core failure modes like incorrect splitting or broken merging logic.
behaviors.test_description: Create or load a multi-hour transcript (>60 minutes) with clear segment boundaries and distinct topics. Verify that the service splits it into segments (each ≤60 minutes). Mock the speech-to-text and LLM APIs to return realistic outputs for each segment (e.g., summaries with action items and decisions). Verify that segments are processed sequentially, and that the final merged summary: (1) contains all action items and decisions from all segments, (2) has no duplication, (3) is logically coherent without jarring context switches, and (4) preserves speaker attributions across segments.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — upload audio, transcribe, summarize, deliver structured output — completes without manual intervention for a real meeting recording.
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires coordinating multiple agentic steps (speech-to-text transcription + LLM summarization) in sequence across real infrastructure (file upload/storage, API calls, output delivery). The failure modes span multiple concerns: API timeouts, network errors, partial failures at any stage, coordination issues between steps, or LLM non-determinism causing the pipeline to fail on some runs. A successful test requires executing the real pipeline end-to-end without mocks for the external services. This is a multi-step agentic workflow: each step depends on the prior one, and correctness depends on real LLM and external API behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Upload a real meeting recording or high-quality synthetic audio (at least 10–20 minutes, multi-speaker) to the service. Trigger the full pipeline: (1) file storage/retrieval, (2) speech-to-text API call, (3) LLM summarization, (4) structured output generation. Verify that all stages complete without errors, within expected time limits (no hangs/timeouts), and that the final structured summary is returned successfully. The output must be well-formed JSON with all expected fields (decisions, action items, attendees). Run the test multiple times (at least 5) to account for transient failures and LLM non-determinism. The test passes only if the pipeline completes successfully on all runs.
